questions.questionText: A person weighs 90 kg and is 1.70 m tall. What is the BMI?
questions.explanation: BMI = 90 / 1.70² = 90 / 2.89 = 31.1 kg/m², which is obese.

questions.questionText: A person weighs 72 kg and is 1.75 m tall. What is the BMI classification?
questions.explanation: BMI = 72 / 1.75² = 72 / 3.06 = 23.5 kg/m². This is within the healthy range 18.5–24.9.

questions.questionText: A person has BMI = 29.5 kg/m². Which interpretation is correct?
questions.explanation: BMI 25–29.9 is overweight. A BMI of 29.5 is near the obesity cutoff of 30.

questions.questionText: A person weighs 120 kg and is 1.70 m tall. What is the BMI and classification?
questions.explanation: BMI = 120 / 1.70² = 120 / 2.89 = 41.5 kg/m², which is over 40 and therefore extremely obese.

questions.questionText: A female patient has waist circumference 88 cm and hip circumference 100 cm. What is her waist-to-hip ratio and classification?
questions.explanation: WHR = 88 / 100 = 0.88. In women, WHR >0.8 indicates apple-shaped/android obesity.

questions.questionText: A male patient has waist circumference 95 cm and hip circumference 105 cm. What is his waist-to-hip ratio and body-shape category?
questions.explanation: WHR = 95 / 105 = 0.90. In men, WHR <1.0 indicates pear-shaped/gynoid distribution.

questions.questionText: A male patient has waist 110 cm and hip 100 cm. What is the best interpretation?
options: WHR 1.1, apple-shaped obesity with higher metabolic risk
questions.explanation: WHR = 110 / 100 = 1.1. In men, WHR >1.0 indicates apple-shaped obesity, linked to higher metabolic risk.

questions.questionText: Two patients have the same BMI of 32 kg/m². Patient A has upper body obesity; Patient B has lower body obesity. Which patient is at higher metabolic risk based on the file?
options: Both have identical risk regardless of fat distribution
Risk cannot be affected by fat distribution
questions.explanation: Upper body/apple-shaped obesity and visceral fat accumulation are associated with higher metabolic disease risk.

questions.questionText: A woman has BMI 31 kg/m² and WHR 0.76. What is the best interpretation?
options: Obese BMI with pear-shaped lower body fat distribution
questions.explanation: BMI ≥30 indicates obesity. WHR <0.8 in women indicates pear-shaped/lower body distribution.

questions.questionText: A man has BMI 27 kg/m² and WHR 1.08. What is the best interpretation?
questions.explanation: BMI 27 is overweight. WHR >1.0 in men indicates apple-shaped obesity, associated with higher risk.

questions.questionText: Which patient would be expected to have the highest metabolic risk?
questions.explanation: Obesity plus upper body/apple-shaped distribution indicates higher metabolic risk.

questions.questionText: A patient has high abdominal fat and high free fatty acid availability. Which adipose tissue behavior best explains this?
options: High lipolysis in abdominal subcutaneous and visceral depots
Complete inhibition of lipolysis in visceral fat
Slow mobilization from gluteal-femoral depots only
questions.explanation: In obese subjects, abdominal subcutaneous and visceral depots show high lipolysis and contribute to increased free fatty acids.

questions.questionText: A patient loses weight successfully. Which adipocyte change is most likely responsible for most of the weight loss?
options: Complete loss of fat cell number
questions.explanation: Weight loss mainly decreases adipocyte size, while adipocyte number may remain increased.

questions.questionText: A patient with prolonged overnutrition reaches the expansion limit of existing adipocytes. What happens next?
options: Preadipocytes proliferate and differentiate into mature adipocytes
questions.explanation: When fat cells reach their size limit, additional weight gain involves recruitment and proliferation of preadipocytes.

questions.questionText: A person has gained modest weight but is not obese. According to the diagram, what mainly changes?
questions.explanation: Modest weight gain/loss in a non-obese person mainly affects adipocyte size, not number.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why visceral fat is more harmful than gluteal-femoral fat?
options: Visceral adipocytes are more metabolically active and contribute to free fatty acid availability
Gluteal-femoral fat has the highest lipolysis rate
Lower body fat is always linked to higher metabolic disease risk
questions.explanation: Visceral adipocytes are highly metabolically active and abdominal/visceral depots have high lipolysis rates.

questions.questionText: A patient has mainly gluteal-femoral fat deposition. Which statement best matches this pattern?
options: Fatty acids tend to mobilize more slowly
It is always the highest-risk obesity pattern
It is defined by WHR >1.0 in women
questions.explanation: Lower-body subcutaneous adipocytes are efficient at fat deposition and tend to mobilize fatty acids more slowly.

questions.questionText: A clinician wants a simple bedside index to identify obesity-related metabolic risk beyond BMI. Which is most useful from the file?
questions.explanation: WHR helps identify apple vs pear fat distribution, which affects metabolic risk.

questions.questionText: A patient has BMI 41 kg/m² but a low waist-to-hip ratio. Which statement is most accurate?
options: BMI indicates extreme obesity, but fat distribution still modifies metabolic risk
BMI is healthy because WHR is low
BMI cannot be calculated if WHR is available
questions.explanation: BMI classifies overall obesity severity, while WHR gives additional information about body fat distribution and metabolic risk.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement is most accurate?
options: Obesity risk depends not only on total fat amount but also on fat distribution and adipocyte biology
Pear-shaped obesity is always more harmful than apple-shaped obesity
Weight loss mainly removes all adipocytes permanently
questions.explanation: The file emphasizes BMI, body fat distribution, visceral/subcutaneous depots, lipolysis differences, and adipocyte size/number as important determinants of obesity risk.
